ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for slipper lobster or salted egg slipper lobster; closest parent crustacean food product used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers lobster and similar crustaceans; no specific slipper lobster or salted egg preparation code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for slipper lobster or salted egg crustacean preparation.

main_ingredients: slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis or Ibacus spp.)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salted egg slipper lobster is approximately 350 g, representing one whole or half slipper lobster coated in salted egg sauce, served as a shared zi char dish.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Spiny lobster, cooked (NDB 15149) and salted duck egg yolk composition; adapted for salted egg sauce preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD crustacean and egg data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for salted egg slipper lobster exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by combining USDA spiny lobster cooked profile (NDB 15149, ~112 kcal/100g, protein ~20.6g, fat ~1.5g, carbs ~2.5g) with a salted egg sauce contribution (butter, salted duck egg yolk, evaporated milk, curry leaves). The sauce adds approximately 40–60 kcal/100g, 3–5g fat, 1–2g protein, and 1–2g carbohydrate per 100g of finished dish. Final per-100g values represent the composite dish at approximately 60% crustacean flesh and 40% sauce by weight. Sodium is elevated due to salted duck egg yolk. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood and protein-dominant dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked crustacean and egg-based sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg slipper lobster. Slipper lobster is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate crustacean. The salted egg sauce adds minimal carbohydrate (butter, egg yolk, evaporated milk). Total carbohydrate per 100g is approximately 3.8g. By analogy with other cooked crustacean and egg-based dishes, GI is estimated at 5 (very low), consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. GL per 350g serving = (5 × 13.3g available carbs) / 100 = 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted egg slipper lobster due to its high sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100g, or ~2380 mg per restaurant serving), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted egg slipper lobster has a very low carbohydrate content (~3.8g per 100g) and an estimated glycemic index of 5, making it a low-glycemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion size should be monitored due to the calorie-dense salted egg sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from salted duck egg yolk and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The salted egg sauce contains butter and salted duck egg yolk, contributing approximately 4.2g saturated fat and 185mg cholesterol per 100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and be mindful of the high sodium content which compounds cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Slipper Lobster is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis or Ibacus spp.) — a flat, fan-shaped crustacean distinct from true lobsters — coated in a rich, creamy salted egg sauce. The sauce is made by stir-frying salted duck egg yolks with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, and chilli in a wok, producing a golden, foamy, intensely savoury coating that clings to the crustacean. The dish is characterised by its bold umami flavour, buttery richness, and a subtle heat from the chilli and curry leaves. The slipper lobster flesh is firm, sweet, and slightly chewy, contrasting with the velvety sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~16.5g per 100g) and moderate in fat (~8.5g per 100g), with elevated sodium (~680mg per 100g) due to the salted duck egg yolk. Cholesterol content is notable (~185mg per 100g). Carbohydrate content is very low (~3.8g per 100g), giving the dish a negligible glycemic impact. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the low carbohydrate profile makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. The dish is typically served as a shared main course in zi char restaurants across Malaysia and Singapore, often priced as a premium seafood item.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for salted egg slipper lobster exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA spiny lobster cooked profile with a modelled salted egg sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate based on the very low carbohydrate content of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from USDA is well-established and the sauce composition is well-characterised.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA spiny lobster source entry and is not reliably estimated for this preparation. All other -1 fields in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values where component-level data permitted. Sodium value (~680mg/100g) is elevated due to salted duck egg yolk, which is a primary ingredient; this is consistent with published salted egg sauce compositions. Cholesterol (~185mg/100g) reflects both crustacean flesh and egg yolk contributions. The dish is not certified halal as it is typically served in non-halal zi char establishments and may involve pork lard in some preparations; is_halal is set to false. GI is estimated at 5 (very low) by analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked crustacean dishes per the University of Sydney GI Database framework. GL per 350g serving calculated as (5 × 13.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.7. The food_id GD003219 is preserved from the input seed record.
